Plant/Drug: Cochlospermum planchonii — yellow cotton tree — malaria (Ghana)

West African traditional medicine — documented from ethnobotanical surveys across Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso. Write a comprehensive entry for this plant.
results.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY

Plant: Cochlospermum planchonii Hook.f. ex Planch. (family Bixaceae / Cochlospermaceae)
Common English name: Yellow cotton tree; West African yellow cotton tree
Local vernacular names:
  — Yoruba (Nigeria): 'Pápá' or 'Igunmu'; also recorded as 'Aborisa'
  — Hausa (Nigeria / Niger / northern Ghana): 'Rawaya' (lit. 'the yellow one'), sometimes 'Tsadar masar' in wider Sahelian usage
  — Twi (Ghana): 'Kokoo ahoma' or 'Kyenekyene'
  — Wolof (Senegal): 'Diwël' or 'Dioulé'
  — Dioula / Bambara (Mali / Côte d'Ivoire / Burkina Faso): 'Boulounguê' or 'Balonge'
  — Mooré (Burkina Faso): 'Koanga'

Botanical description and distribution:
Cochlospermum planchonii is a small to medium-sized deciduous savanna shrub or subshrub, typically 0.5–2 m in height, arising from a large, tuberous, woody rootstock that persists through fire and dry season. It is distributed across the Guinea and Sudan savanna zones of West Africa, from Senegal and Gambia eastward through Mali, Burkina Faso, Ghana, and Nigeria into Cameroon. The plant is characteristically found in open woodland, fallow farmland, and rocky lateritic soils. It produces bright yellow, five-petalled flowers (hence the common name) before or alongside the emergence of deeply lobed, palmate leaves. The mature fruit is a capsule containing cottony fibres surrounding the seeds — a morphological feature shared with the closely related C. tinctorium and C. religiosum (the Asian silk-cotton). Roots are starchy, yellowish-orange in cross-section due to the presence of arjunolic acid and cochlospermins.

Ethnobotanical and therapeutic uses:

1. MALARIA (primary documented use — Ghana, Burkina Faso, Mali, Nigeria)
The root and root bark of C. planchonii constitute the most widely documented antimalarial remedy in the Guinea-Sudan savanna belt. In Ghana, ethnobotanical surveys conducted in the northern, upper-east, and Brong-Ahafo regions consistently record the plant as a first-line traditional treatment for uncomplicated febrile malaria, particularly in peri-urban and rural communities without ready access to biomedical facilities.
Preparation method (decoction): The dried or fresh roots, or root bark, are cut into small pieces, boiled in water for 20–45 minutes until a dark amber-to-orange decoction is produced, then cooled and strained. Adults typically consume one to two cups (approximately 200–400 mL) of the decoction two to three times daily for three to five days. In some Hausa-speaking communities of northern Nigeria and Niger, the decoction is prepared from roots combined with those of Guiera senegalensis J.F.Gmel. (Combretaceae) to enhance efficacy, a preparation locally called 'zogale malaira.'
Preparation method (infusion): In Sahelian communities (Senegal, Mali), the dried root is sometimes cold-infused in water overnight and drunk in the morning as a prophylactic tonic during the wet (transmission) season.
Preparation method (combined decoction): In Burkina Faso (Mossi communities), the roots of C. planchonii are combined with the stem bark of Khaya senegalensis (Desr.) A.Juss. and leaves of Azadirachta indica A.Juss. in a compound decoction used for severe febrile episodes attributed to malaria.

2. JAUNDICE AND HEPATIC CONDITIONS (Ghana, Côte d'Ivoire, Burkina Faso)
The root decoction is widely used across multiple ethnic groups for the treatment of jaundice [icterus], hepatitis-like syndromes locally described as 'yellow eyes' or 'yellow sickness,' and general hepatomegaly. In Twi-speaking communities of Ghana, a root decoction is drunk twice daily for one to two weeks for conditions described as 'liver sickness.' In Côte d'Ivoire, the treatment is sometimes administered alongside dietary restriction of palm oil and fats.

3. ANAEMIA AND HAEMATINIC USE (Nigeria, Burkina Faso)
The root decoction is consumed as a general haematinic [blood tonic] in communities across northern Nigeria and Burkina Faso, particularly for post-febrile anaemia and general debility following illness. The perceived mechanism in traditional explanatory frameworks relates to the orange-yellow colour of the root, interpreted through colour-sympathy doctrine as beneficial to blood production.

4. FEVER AND ANTIPYRETIC USE (widespread)
Across all surveyed countries, the root decoction or cold infusion is used as a general antipyretic for undifferentiated febrile illness in adults and children. In children, diluted decoctions are administered; tepid sponging with the decoction is also recorded in Hausa communities.

5. GASTROINTESTINAL CONDITIONS (Nigeria, Ghana)
In Yoruba-speaking southwestern Nigeria, the root decoction is used for dysentery and diarrhoea. In Ghana (northern regions), a weaker decoction is drunk for stomach pain and indigestion.

6. WOUND HEALING AND TOPICAL USE (Mali, Senegal)
A poultice prepared from the fresh or dried root, ground with water to a paste, is applied directly to wounds, skin infections, and oedematous swellings in Mali and Senegal. The paste is also applied to the forehead and temples as an external antipyretic.

Pharmacological and phytochemical research:
Cochlospermum planchonii has attracted substantial pharmacological investigation, particularly in relation to its antimalarial claims. The following represents a synthesis of published research findings as archived in the Plantacopia corpus:

— Phytochemical constituents: Major identified compounds include arjunolic acid (a pentacyclic triterpenoid), cochlospermins A and B (guaiane-type sesquiterpenes), flavonoids (kaempferol, quercetin glycosides), phenolic acids (gallic acid, ellagic acid), and tannins. The yellow-orange pigmentation of the root is attributable principally to arjunolic acid derivatives and to the presence of cochliodines.

— Antimalarial activity: In vitro studies using Plasmodium falciparum cultures (chloroquine-sensitive and chloroquine-resistant strains, including K1 and 3D7 strains) have demonstrated significant antiplasmodial activity of root extracts and isolated fractions. IC50 values for crude aqueous and ethanolic extracts reported in multiple studies range from approximately 0.8 to 12 µg/mL, with purified arjunolic acid fractions showing IC50 values in the low microgram range. In vivo murine models (P. berghei ANKA in BALB/c mice) confirm significant parasite suppression at oral doses of 100–400 mg/kg body weight. Clinical surveys (observational, non-randomised) in Ghana and Burkina Faso have recorded high patient-reported efficacy for uncomplicated malaria, though randomised controlled clinical trials as of the time of corpus compilation remain limited.

— Hepatoprotective activity: Hepatoprotective properties have been demonstrated in rodent models of CCl4-induced hepatotoxicity, with extracts significantly reducing serum ALT, AST, and bilirubin levels relative to controls, consistent with the traditional use for jaundice and hepatic conditions.

— Antipyretic and anti-inflammatory activity: Aqueous and methanol root extracts have demonstrated significant antipyretic effects in yeast-induced hyperthermia models and anti-inflammatory activity in carrageenan-induced paw oedema models in rodents, supporting the traditional antipyretic use.

— Toxicological profile: Acute toxicity studies in rodents report LD50 values for oral administration generally exceeding 2000 mg/kg body weight, suggesting a relatively wide therapeutic margin for traditional use doses. Sub-chronic toxicity studies at repeated doses show mild hepatic and renal changes at very high doses; at doses within the traditional preparation range, no significant organ toxicity has been reported.

Notes on related species and potential confusion:
C. planchonii is frequently confused in field surveys and local nomenclature with the closely related C. tinctorium A.Rich., which shares many vernacular names (particularly in Hausa, where both may be called 'rawaya') and is used for largely overlapping therapeutic purposes. Some researchers have suggested that a significant proportion of the ethnobotanical literature and pharmacological studies attributed to C. planchonii may involve C. tinctorium, as morphological differentiation in the field — particularly of roots — is difficult without mature floral material. Plantacopia entries treat these as distinct taxa pending further herbarium verification but note this important caveat.

Conservation and sustainability:
Harvesting pressure on C. planchonii is considered moderate to high in areas of intensive traditional medical practice, as the entire root system is typically extracted, killing the individual plant. Regeneration from seed is possible but slow. Cultivation trials have been initiated in Burkina Faso and Mali as part of traditional medicine sustainability programmes.

References (representative; see full Plantacopia bibliography):
Asase A. et al. (2005) — Ghana ethnobotanical surveys. Pousset J.-L. (1989) — Plantes médicinales africaines. Soh PN et al. (2009) — antimalarial activity. Diallo D. et al. — Mali traditional medicine documentation. Nigerian Natural Medicine Development Agency surveys (various years). TRAMIL network documentation (Côte d'Ivoire node).

Plant/Drug: Cassia sieberiana — African laburnum — jaundice, liver

West African traditional medicine — documented from ethnobotanical surveys across Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso. Write a comprehensive entry for this plant.
results.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY

Plant: Cassia sieberiana DC.
Family: Fabaceae (Caesalpinioideae)
Common Name: African Laburnum; African Wisteria Tree; Monkey Pod

LOCAL NAMES
• Yoruba (Nigeria): 'ìdòfún', 'akàsá'
• Hausa (Nigeria/Niger): 'marga', 'marga-marga'
• Twi (Ghana): 'asantewa', 'prekese-kɔkɔɔ'
• Wolof (Senegal): 'sɛnɛ', 'diabakhou'
• Bambara/Dioula (Mali/Côte d'Ivoire): 'ntorossi', 'toro'
• Mooré (Burkina Faso): 'tiim-puuga'

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION
Cassia sieberiana is a medium-to-large deciduous tree reaching 10–20 m in height, widespread across the Sudano-Sahelian and Guinea savanna zones of West Africa. It is distinguished by its long, pendulous, cylindrical seed pods (30–60 cm), which darken to blackish-brown at maturity and contain a sweet-smelling dark pulp surrounding individual seeds. The bark is grey-brown, fissured, and strongly bitter. Leaves are pinnate with 8–14 pairs of leaflets; flowers are yellow and borne in axillary racemes.

PARTS USED
Root bark (most commonly), stem bark, leaves, roots, seed pulp.

THERAPEUTIC USES AND CONDITIONS TREATED

1. Jaundice and Hepatobiliary Disorders
The most widely documented use of Cassia sieberiana across all six survey regions is the treatment of jaundice [Hausa: 'rawaya'; Yoruba: 'àárùn ẹ̀dọ̀'], a condition associated locally with liver dysfunction, bile obstruction, and in some frameworks, spiritual causation. Traditional healers [Hausa: 'boka'; Yoruba: 'onísègùn'] administer decoctions of the root bark as the primary hepatoprotective [liver-protective] intervention.

Preparation (Decoction): Sections of dried or fresh root bark (approximately 50–100 g) are vigorously boiled in 1–2 litres of water for 20–40 minutes until the liquor becomes deep brown-orange. The decoction is strained and administered orally, typically as one cup (approximately 200–250 mL) two to three times daily for seven to fourteen days. In Senegal and Mali, the root bark is sometimes macerated [cold-soaked] in water overnight rather than decocted, producing a lighter preparation considered suitable for milder presentations.

2. General Liver Disease and Hepatomegaly [Enlarged Liver]
In Ghana (Brong-Ahafo and Northern regions) and Burkina Faso, healers use the stem bark decoction for patients presenting with abdominal swelling attributed to an enlarged or diseased liver. The preparation method mirrors the root bark decoction above, though some practitioners combine it with Khaya senegalensis [African mahogany] bark.

3. Constipation and Intestinal Parasites
The seed pulp — analogous in chemistry to that of other Cassia species — is used as a mild laxative [purgative] across all survey regions. The pulp is consumed directly or dissolved in warm water. In Nigeria (Hausa-speaking north), the root bark decoction is additionally administered for intestinal worms [helminthiasis], with healers noting its bitterness as an indicator of antiparasitic potency.

4. Malaria and Febrile Illness
In Côte d'Ivoire and Mali, leaf decoctions or root bark decoctions are given for fevers, including those attributed to malaria [Bambara: 'sumaya']. The preparation is administered orally; in some cases, patients are also bathed with the cooled decoction to reduce body temperature.

5. Skin Conditions
Poultices of pounded fresh leaves or ground root bark are applied topically to skin eruptions, fungal infections, and wounds across Ghana and Senegal. The material is applied directly to the affected area and bound with cloth; renewed daily.

6. Sexually Transmitted Infections and Urinary Complaints
In Nigeria and Burkina Faso, root bark decoctions are administered for urethral discharge and dysuria [painful urination], with some healers specifically citing gonorrhoea [Hausa: 'jikin biri'] as a target condition.

PREPARATION METHODS SUMMARY
• Decoction (root bark or stem bark): Most common; used for jaundice, liver disease, malaria, fever, parasites, STIs.
• Maceration (cold infusion, root bark): Used in Senegal and Mali; milder hepatic [liver] complaints.
• Poultice (fresh leaves or powdered root bark): Used topically for dermatological conditions and wounds.
• Direct ingestion (seed pulp): Used as laxative/purgative.
• Bath (cooled leaf or bark decoction): Febrile illness, body temperature management.

PHARMACOLOGICAL AND PHYTOCHEMICAL RESEARCH
Phytochemical investigations of Cassia sieberiana have identified the following classes of secondary metabolites:
• Anthraquinones (including sennosides, rhein, emodin): Consistent with the documented laxative and antiparasitic uses; well-established mechanisms in related Cassia/Senna species.
• Cassane-type diterpenoids and flavonoids: Identified in bark and root extracts; relevant to anti-inflammatory activity.
• Tannins and saponins: Present in bark; contribute to astringent and antimicrobial properties.
• Alkaloids: Detected in root bark at low concentrations.

Key pharmacological findings from peer-reviewed literature include:
• Hepatoprotective activity: Ethanolic and aqueous root bark extracts demonstrated significant hepatoprotective effects in CCl₄-induced [carbon tetrachloride-induced] hepatotoxicity models in rats, with reductions in serum ALT, AST, and bilirubin levels, lending direct biochemical support to the ethnobotanical use for jaundice and liver disease (Koné et al., 2004; Asare et al., 2011).
• Antimicrobial activity: Extracts showed inhibition of Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, and Candida albicans in vitro, partially validating uses for skin and urinary infections.
• Antiplasmodial activity: Moderate in vitro activity against Plasmodium falciparum has been reported, consistent with antimalarial ethnobotanical claims, though clinical efficacy data remain limited.
• Antioxidant activity: Root bark extracts demonstrated DPPH [2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl] radical scavenging activity, which may contribute to hepatoprotective mechanisms.

TOXICOLOGY AND SAFETY NOTES
High doses of anthraquinone-containing preparations carry risks of intestinal cramping, electrolyte imbalance, and, with prolonged use, hepatotoxicity paradoxically — a concern noted in the broader Cassia literature. Traditional healers in all survey regions consistently emphasised short treatment courses (typically 7–14 days) and moderate dosage, suggesting indigenous recognition of dose-dependent risk. No formal clinical toxicity studies specific to C. sieberiana in humans are available as of the time of survey compilation.

CONSERVATION STATUS
Cassia sieberiana is currently not listed as threatened; however, intensifying harvest of root bark for medicinal trade — particularly in peri-urban markets in Accra, Lagos, Dakar, and Ouagadougou — has raised concerns among some survey respondents regarding local population pressure on mature trees.

SOURCES AND SURVEY REGIONS
Documented from ethnobotanical field surveys: Ghana (Northern, Brong-Ahafo, Volta regions), Nigeria (Kano, Sokoto, Kaduna states; Oyo, Osun states), Senegal (Casamance, Sine-Saloum), Mali (Ségou, Mopti), Côte d'Ivoire (Korhogo, Bouaké zones), Burkina Faso (Sahel and Centre-Nord regions).

Cross-referenced with: Kerharo & Adam (1974), Burkill (1995), Neuwinger (2000), Dalziel (1937), and primary survey field notes.

Plant/Drug: Afzelia africana — African mahogany alternative — pain

West African traditional medicine — documented from ethnobotanical surveys across Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso. Write a comprehensive entry for this plant.
results.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY

Afzelia africana Sm. ex Pers. (Fabaceae / Caesalpinioideae)

Common and Local Names:
— Yoruba (Nigeria): Apa, Akpakpa
— Hausa (Nigeria/Niger): Kawo, Dorowa-masar
— Twi (Ghana): Papao, Papao-nua
— Wolof (Senegal): Lingue, Lañ
— Dioula/Bambara (Mali, Burkina Faso, Côte d'Ivoire): Lingué, Bàlà
— French regional vernacular: Lingué, Doussié rouge
— English trade/vernacular: African mahogany (alternative designation; not to be confused with Khaya spp.), Apa, Aligna

Botanical Description and Distribution:
Afzelia africana is a large, deciduous tree of the subfamily Caesalpinioideae, attaining heights of 15–30 m, with a broad spreading crown and deeply fissured, grey-brown bark. The tree bears pinnate leaves with 4–5 pairs of ovate-elliptic leaflets, and produces distinctive red-and-white flowers in terminal panicles. The fruit is a large, woody, indehiscent or tardily dehiscent pod containing hard, black seeds with a conspicuous orange-red aril. It is widely distributed across the Sudano-Guinean savannah zone and semi-deciduous forest margins from Senegal east through Mali, Burkina Faso, Ghana, and Nigeria. The timber is commercially valued and the species is listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List owing to overharvesting.

Ethnobotanical Survey Data (West Africa — Multi-Country):

1. PAIN MANAGEMENT AND ANALGESIC USE
[Principal therapeutic application across surveyed regions]

Conditions Treated:
— General musculoskeletal pain, arthralgia [joint pain], lumbago [lower back pain], headache, toothache, odontogenic pain, post-partum pain, and traumatic/wound pain.
— In Nigeria (Yoruba communities), bark decoctions are the primary modality for managing chronic joint and back pain.
— In Ghana (Brong-Ahafo and Northern regions), root bark preparations are applied both internally and externally for pain of rheumatic origin.
— In Senegal (Wolof-speaking communities), a bark macerate [cold-water extract left to soak] is consumed for headache and febrile pain.
— In Mali and Burkina Faso, stem bark decoctions serve as analgesic preparations among Bambara and Mossi practitioners.

Preparation Methods — Analgesic:
(a) Decoction [internal]: Stem bark or root bark (50–100 g dried material) is boiled in 1–2 litres of water for 20–30 minutes, strained, and administered orally as a warm drink, typically 1–2 cups daily until symptoms resolve.
(b) Maceration [internal/external]: Bark pieces are soaked in cold water overnight; the resulting infusion is either drunk or used as a wash over painful areas.
(c) Poultice [external]: Fresh or powdered bark mixed with shea butter (Vitellaria paradoxa) or palm oil is applied topically to painful joints, the lower back, or the site of injury and left in place for several hours.
(d) Steam inhalation: In some Ghanaian and Malian accounts, boiling bark decoctions are inhaled for headache relief.

2. FEVER AND MALARIA

Conditions Treated:
— Febrile illness, malaria [locally designated 'fever sickness' or palu], and chills.

Preparation Methods — Antipyretic/Antimalarial:
(a) Bark decoction (as above): Administered orally; often combined with Neem (Azadirachta indica) bark or Lemon grass (Cymbopogon citratus) in polyherbal preparations.
(b) Root decoction: Roots boiled and taken as a tonic drink twice daily during febrile episodes.

3. WOUND HEALING AND DERMATOLOGICAL CONDITIONS

Conditions Treated:
— Wounds, ulcers [chronic skin ulcers], skin infections, and boils.

Preparation Methods — Vulnerary [wound-healing]:
(a) Powdered bark: Dried bark ground to a fine powder and applied directly to wounds or ulcerated surfaces as a styptic [bleeding arrest] and antiseptic agent.
(b) Bark ash: Ash from burned bark dissolved in water or palm oil used as a topical wound dressing, particularly in rural Burkina Faso and northern Ghana.
(c) Leaf poultice: Crushed fresh leaves applied to minor cuts and abrasions.

4. GASTROINTESTINAL CONDITIONS

Conditions Treated:
— Diarrhoea, dysentery [locally designated 'stooling sickness'], stomach pain, intestinal parasites.

Preparation Methods — Antidiarrhoeal/Anthelmintic:
(a) Bark decoction (as above, lower concentration): Taken orally in small doses for diarrhoea and intestinal complaints.
(b) Root infusion: Cold macerate of roots taken for intestinal worms, documented among Hausa practitioners in northern Nigeria.

5. GYNAECOLOGICAL AND OBSTETRIC USES

Conditions Treated:
— Post-partum uterine pain, menstrual irregularity, and facilitation of labour.

Preparation Methods:
(a) Bark decoction: Administered to postpartum women to relieve uterine pain and promote recovery; dosage and duration carefully managed by traditional birth attendants.
(b) Seed aril preparation: The orange aril surrounding the seed occasionally used in tonics for general female reproductive health.

6. ANTIMICROBIAL AND ANTI-INFLAMMATORY APPLICATIONS

Conditions Treated:
— Respiratory infections, urinary tract infections, inflammatory swellings.

Preparation Methods:
(a) Bark decoction/infusion used orally for respiratory tract infections (cough, bronchitis).
(b) Topical bark paste applied for inflammatory swellings and lymphadenopathy [swollen lymph nodes].

Pharmacological and Phytochemical Research:

Phytochemical Constituents:
Afzelia africana bark and other plant parts have been documented to contain:
— Tannins (hydrolysable and condensed) — astringent and antimicrobial properties.
— Flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol derivatives) — antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity.
— Saponins — implicated in antimicrobial and haemolytic activity.
— Terpenoids and sterols — potential analgesic and anti-inflammatory action.
— Afzelechin and other flavan-3-ols — isolated from seeds and bark.
— Alkaloids (trace quantities) — reported in some analyses.

Pharmacological Studies (Supporting Research):
1. Analgesic activity: Ethanolic and aqueous bark extracts have demonstrated significant analgesic effects in rodent models (acetic acid-induced writhing test; hot-plate test), consistent with traditional use for pain management. Studies conducted at Nigerian and Ghanaian research institutions (e.g., University of Ibadan, Kwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology) have documented dose-dependent antinociceptive [pain-reducing] effects.
2. Anti-inflammatory activity: Bark extracts inhibit carrageenan-induced paw oedema in rat models, supporting anti-inflammatory ethnopharmacological claims. The activity is attributed in part to flavonoid and tannin fractions.
3. Antimicrobial activity: In vitro studies demonstrate inhibitory activity against Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Candida albicans, corroborating wound-healing and anti-infective uses.
4. Antipyretic activity: Bark extracts exhibit antipyretic effects in yeast-induced pyrexia models in rats, lending support to the fever management applications documented across all surveyed countries.
5. Antiplasmodial potential: Limited in vitro screening suggests moderate activity against Plasmodium falciparum, though clinical data remain absent; further research is recommended.
6. Antioxidant activity: High phenolic content correlates with significant free radical scavenging activity (DPPH assay), consistent with wound-healing and anti-inflammatory functions.

Toxicological Notes:
High-dose administration of bark extracts in animal studies has shown hepatotoxic [liver-toxic] potential, suggesting caution is warranted in prolonged or high-dose traditional use. No systematic human toxicological studies are available as of the time of compilation. Traditional practitioners typically administer decoctions in moderate, time-limited doses.

Conservation Status and Sustainability Note:
Afzelia africana is classified as Vulnerable (VU) by the IUCN Red List. Bark-stripping for medicinal purposes, combined with commercial timber exploitation and agricultural land clearance, constitutes a significant threat. Ethnobotanical documentation programs in Ghana and Burkina Faso have recommended cultivation of this species in agroforestry systems and promotion of bark harvesting protocols that do not girdle [completely encircle] the trunk, in order to preserve tree viability.

Cross-References:
— Khaya senegalensis (African mahogany, primary designation)
— Pterocarpus erinaceus (Senegalese rosewood; co-used in some analgesic preparations)
— Vitellaria paradoxa (Shea tree; excipient in poultice preparations)
— Azadirachta indica (Neem; co-ingredient in antimalarial compounds)

Survey Sources: Ethnobotanical field surveys, Ghana (Northern and Brong-Ahafo Regions); Nigeria (Oyo, Kwara, Kano States); Senegal (Kaolack, Tambacounda); Mali (Sikasso, Ségou); Côte d'Ivoire (Korhogo, Bouaké); Burkina Faso (Bobo-Dioulasso, Ouagadougou). Survey period: late 20th–early 21st century.

Plant/Drug: Senna alata — Candle bush (ringworm bush) — skin fungal

West African traditional medicine — documented from ethnobotanical surveys across Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso. Write a comprehensive entry for this plant.
results.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY
Taxon: Senna alata (L.) Roxb.
Family: Fabaceae (Caesalpinioideae)
Common Names: Candle Bush; Ringworm Bush; Emperor's Candlesticks

REGIONAL VERNACULAR NAMES
• Yoruba (Nigeria): Asunwon oyinbo; Ogalu
• Hausa (Nigeria / Niger): Tafashiya; Marke
• Twi (Ghana): Osram ne nsoromma ('moon and stars'); Akatawiri
• Wolof (Senegal): Sεnε bu dëkk; Ndoté
• Dioula / Bambara (Mali / Côte d'Ivoire / Burkina Faso): Sô-sô-ni; Fizan
• Fula / Fulfulde: Dabbere
• Ewe (Ghana / Togo): Atimatima

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION
Senna alata is a fast-growing, erect, semi-woody shrub or small tree reaching 1–3 m in height. Its most distinctive feature is the large, erect, spike-like racemes of bright yellow flowers enclosed initially in greenish-orange bracts resembling candles — hence the common name 'Candle Bush.' Leaves are pinnately compound, with 8–20 pairs of oblong leaflets. Pods are winged and dark brown at maturity. The plant is widely naturalised throughout tropical West Africa, commonly found along roadsides, farm margins, stream banks, and disturbed land.

PRIMARY THERAPEUTIC USES
The species is one of the most frequently cited medicinal plants in ethnobotanical surveys across West Africa, principally for dermatological and gastrointestinal conditions.

1. DERMATOLOGICAL CONDITIONS (most documented use)
   • Dermatophytoses [ringworm / tinea infections], including tinea corporis, tinea capitis, and tinea pedis ('athlete's foot')
   • Scabies and mange
   • Eczema and psoriasis-like scaling conditions
   • Impetigo and superficial bacterial skin infections
   • Fungal nail infections [onychomycosis]
   • Leprosy-associated skin lesions (adjunct treatment, historically documented in Mali and Senegal)

2. GASTROINTESTINAL CONDITIONS
   • Constipation — leaves and pods contain anthraquinone glycosides (notably rhein, aloe-emodin, kaempferol) with potent laxative properties; widely employed across the region as a purgative [cathartic]
   • Intestinal parasites / helminthiasis

3. FEBRILE AND SYSTEMIC CONDITIONS
   • Malaria (antipyretic use documented in Ghana, Nigeria, Burkina Faso)
   • General fevers

4. OTHER REPORTED USES
   • Sexually transmitted infections (gonorrhoea) — leaf decoction taken internally (Nigeria, Côte d'Ivoire)
   • Oedema and inflammatory joint conditions — leaf poultice
   • Snake bite — crushed leaf poultice applied locally (Mali, Burkina Faso)
   • Wound healing — fresh leaf juice

PREPARATION METHODS

1. POULTICE (dominant preparation for fungal skin disease)
   • Fresh leaves are harvested, washed, and crushed or macerated using a stone mortar and pestle until a green-black paste is obtained. The paste is applied directly to affected skin areas (particularly ringworm lesions) once or twice daily, left for 15–30 minutes, then rinsed with water. In some Ghanaian and Nigerian practices, a few drops of palm kernel oil or shea butter are incorporated to improve adhesion and potentiate absorption. Treatment course typically 7–21 days depending on severity.

2. LEAF JUICE (direct application)
   • Fresh leaves are squeezed or rubbed directly onto lesions. This is the most rapid and simple preparation, commonly used by rural practitioners for tinea infections in children. The characteristic yellow-green sap is the active medium.

3. AQUEOUS DECOCTION (internal use — purgative and antimalarial)
   • Leaves (fresh or dried), bark, or roots are simmered in water for 20–40 minutes. The decoction is strained and consumed in measured doses — typically one to two cups daily. For constipation, lower doses are used; higher doses are employed as a strong purgative or anthelmintic. Practitioners in Ghana and Nigeria frequently combine this with ginger [Zingiber officinale] to moderate griping.

4. COLD INFUSION (internal use — general tonic)
   • Dried and powdered leaves are steeped in cold or warm (not boiling) water overnight. The infusion is consumed for general debility and low-grade fevers. This method preserves more heat-labile constituents than full decoction.

5. BARK DECOCTION
   • Root bark or stem bark decoction is used in Senegal and Mali for gonorrhoea and urinary tract complaints — one to two cups taken daily for five to seven days.

6. SMOKE / FUMIGATION
   • Dry leaves are burned in some Burkina Faso traditions; smoke is directed toward infected scalp areas for tinea capitis. Considered a secondary method, often combined with topical paste.

ACTIVE PHYTOCHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS
(As identified in supporting laboratory literature)
• Anthraquinones: rhein, aloe-emodin, chrysophanol, physcion — responsible for both antifungal and laxative activity
• Flavonoids: kaempferol, quercetin, isorhamnetin — anti-inflammatory and antioxidant
• Tannins: hydrolysable and condensed — astringent and antimicrobial
• Alkaloids: trace quantities
• Sterols: β-sitosterol
• Volatile fatty acids

SUPPORTING PHARMACOLOGICAL RESEARCH

1. ANTIFUNGAL ACTIVITY
   Multiple in vitro studies confirm significant antifungal activity of Senna alata leaf extracts. Ethnopharmacological work by Damodaran & Venkataraman (1994) and subsequently confirmed by Palanichamy & Nagarajan demonstrated strong inhibition of Trichophyton rubrum, Trichophyton mentagrophytes, Microsporum canis, and Candida albicans — dermatophytes directly implicated in the ringworm conditions treated traditionally. Ethanolic and methanolic leaf extracts consistently outperformed aqueous extracts in minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) assays, suggesting that some key antifungal compounds are not fully water-soluble, though the traditional aqueous preparations remain clinically effective at in-use concentrations.
   
   A controlled clinical study conducted in Thailand (Thaweboon et al., cited in multiple West African pharmacological reviews) compared Senna alata cream to clotrimazole 1% in tinea versicolor patients, finding comparable efficacy after four weeks of twice-daily application — providing rare clinical corroboration for the traditional topical use.

2. ANTIBACTERIAL ACTIVITY
   Leaf extracts show inhibitory activity against Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pyogenes, Escherichia coli, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Abo et al., 1999; Nigerian survey data), supporting use in mixed bacterial-fungal skin infections.

3. LAXATIVE / ANTHRAQUINONE PHARMACOLOGY
   The anthraquinone glycoside content is well characterised and constitutes the pharmacological basis for the documented cathartic use. This is consistent with the broader genus Senna (formerly Cassia), several members of which are official pharmacopoeial laxatives.

4. ANTIMALARIAL ACTIVITY
   In vitro studies demonstrate moderate activity against Plasmodium falciparum. Ethanolic extracts showed IC₅₀ values consistent with weak-to-moderate antimalarial potential, supporting the antipyretic traditional use without fully explaining it.

5. ANTI-INFLAMMATORY ACTIVITY
   Kaempferol and quercetin fractions demonstrate cyclooxygenase inhibition in vitro, supporting anti-inflammatory applications documented for joint and wound conditions.

TOXICOLOGY AND SAFETY NOTES
• The anthraquinone content means that prolonged or excessive internal consumption may cause electrolyte imbalance, hypokalaemia, and intestinal cramping. Traditional practitioners generally limit internal use to short courses.
• Topical use on intact or mildly broken skin is generally regarded as safe at traditional use levels; however, contact sensitisation has been reported in rare cases.
• The plant is classified as a potent cathartic [drastica] and should not be used internally during pregnancy — a caution acknowledged by experienced traditional practitioners in Nigeria and Ghana.

ETHNOBOTANICAL SURVEY SOURCES
Data synthesised from surveys conducted in:
• Ghana: Akan-speaking communities (Ashanti and Brong-Ahafo regions); Volta Region (Ewe communities)
• Nigeria: Yoruba-speaking Southwest Nigeria (Ogun, Oyo, Osun States); Hausa communities (Kano, Kaduna)
• Senegal: Wolof and Serer communities (Thiès and Diourbel regions)
• Mali: Bambara communities (Ségou and Mopti regions)
• Côte d'Ivoire: Dioula-speaking communities (Odienné and Korhogo)
• Burkina Faso: Mossi communities (Centre and Nord regions)

CONSERVATION STATUS
Senna alata is not threatened. It is a pioneer species on disturbed land and is frequently cultivated as an ornamental as well as a medicinal plant. Its abundance and accessibility contribute to its widespread role in primary healthcare across the sub-region.

results.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY

Plant: Detarium microcarpum Guill. & Perr.
Family: Fabaceae (Caesalpinioideae)
Common regional name (French): Dattier du Sénégal [lit. 'Senegalese date-tree']

--- LOCAL NAMES ---
• Yoruba (Nigeria): Oganwo igbo [also recorded as 'tsamiyar yamma' in some Yoruba-Hausa contact zones; attribution uncertain — see translator notes]
• Hausa (Nigeria/Niger): Taura [widely attested; refers specifically to the sweet-pulped form]; also Tsamiyar kaji in some northern Nigerian dialects
• Twi (Ghana): Aduaba or Tweneboa dua [usage varies by region; Brong-Ahafo surveys record 'tweneboa' more frequently for timber context]
• Wolof (Senegal/The Gambia): Dex [also transcribed 'Dëkk' in standardised Wolof orthography]; pulp known as 'dëx bu dëkk'
• Mooré (Burkina Faso): Tiim-pelbga
• Bambara/Dioula (Mali/Côte d'Ivoire): Tamba or N'tamba

--- BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION ---
Detarium microcarpum is a medium to large deciduous tree reaching 15–25 m in height, occurring across the Sudano-Sahelian and Guineo-Sudanian savanna zones of West Africa. The bark is grey-brown, fissured, and fibrous. Leaves are alternate, paripinnate, with 4–8 pairs of elliptic to oblong leaflets. Flowers are small, cream to pale yellow, borne in axillary or terminal panicles. The fruit is a compressed, indehiscent drupe, 3–5 cm in diameter, with a thin, hard shell enclosing a dry, mealy, edible pulp surrounding a single hard seed. The species is closely related to Detarium senegalense J.F.Gmel., which is the larger, forest-margin form with sweeter and more abundant pulp; D. microcarpum is the Sahelian savanna ecotype. The two are sometimes confused in ethnobotanical literature, and some therapeutic attributions may overlap or have been conflated across surveys.

--- THERAPEUTIC USES AND CONDITIONS TREATED ---

1. DYSENTERY AND DIARRHOEAL DISEASES [PRIMARY DOCUMENTED USE]
Detarium microcarpum is most consistently recorded across all six surveyed countries as a remedy for dysentery [amoebic and bacillary], acute and chronic diarrhoea, and gastrointestinal infections broadly. This use is documented in Burkina Faso (IRSS ethnobotanical surveys, 1990s–2000s), Mali (Pharmacopée du Mali), northern Ghana (Upper West and Northern Regions), and Senegal (Casamance and Sine-Saloum surveys).

   Preparation (bark decoction — most common): The root bark or stem bark is harvested, cleaned, and cut into fragments. A decoction [Fr. décoction; Hausa: ruwan magani] is prepared by simmering approximately 50–100 g of fresh bark (or 20–40 g dried) in 500–750 mL of water for 20–30 minutes. The liquid is strained and cooled. Administered orally, typically 100–150 mL two to three times daily until symptoms resolve, usually for 3–5 days. Some practitioners add potash [kanwa, trona] or salt to the decoction as a preservative and taste-modifier.

   Preparation (leaf infusion — secondary): Fresh or dried leaves are steeped in boiled water for 10–15 minutes [infusion rather than decoction, preserving more volatile constituents]. Used similarly for mild diarrhoea; considered less potent than the bark preparation by most informants.

   Preparation (fruit pulp — dietary/therapeutic overlap): The dried, powdered pulp is mixed with water or gruel [bouillie, tô] and consumed. This preparation is regarded as gentler, suitable for children and convalescent patients, and serves both nutritional and antidiarrhoeal purposes.

2. WOUND HEALING AND SKIN CONDITIONS
Poultice preparations using the powdered bark or leaf paste are applied to wounds, ulcers, and skin infections including fungal dermatoses. In Burkina Faso (Mouhoun Province surveys), fresh leaf pulp is macerated and applied directly to infected cuts. In Senegal, a paste of powdered bark mixed with shea butter [karité, Vitellaria paradoxa] is applied to scabies lesions and chronic skin ulcers.

   Preparation (poultice): Bark or leaves are ground to a paste using a stone mortar, optionally mixed with a binding agent (shea butter, palm oil, or water). Applied topically to the affected area, covered with a cloth or leaf wrap, and changed once or twice daily.

3. FEVER AND MALARIA
Decoctions of the root bark are used in northern Ghana (Sissala and Dagaaba communities) and Burkina Faso (Mossi communities) for febrile illness attributed to malaria [palu]. The preparation method is essentially identical to the dysentery bark decoction. Some healers specify that the root bark is preferred over stem bark for antipyretic use, citing stronger bitterness [attributed to higher alkaloid/tannin content by informants, consistent with phytochemical findings].

4. ORAL HEALTH
Chewing sticks [bâtons à mâcher] prepared from young branches are used across Sahelian West Africa for dental hygiene and the treatment of toothache, gingivitis, and mouth ulcers. This use is documented in Mali, Senegal, and Burkina Faso.

5. RESPIRATORY CONDITIONS
In Mali (Mopti and Ségou Regions) and Côte d'Ivoire (northern districts), steam inhalation over a decoction of leaves and bark is used for coughs, bronchitis, and chest congestion. The patient inhales steam from a covered vessel for 5–10 minutes.

6. FEMALE REPRODUCTIVE HEALTH
In Senegal (Casamance surveys) and Burkina Faso, bark decoctions are used by traditional birth attendants [matrones] for postpartum recovery, to manage lochia, and reportedly to stimulate uterine contraction. Use during pregnancy is noted but cautioned against by some informants; this caution is consistent with the documented presence of saponins and tannins with potential uterotonic activity.

7. NUTRITIONAL AND TONIC USES
The fruit pulp is consumed widely as a food and tonic, particularly by children and nursing mothers. It is reported to be high in ascorbic acid (Vitamin C), iron, and dietary fibre, contributing to its use in managing anaemia and general debility.

--- PHARMACOLOGICAL AND PHYTOCHEMICAL RESEARCH ---

Phytochemical investigations of Detarium microcarpum have identified the following classes of secondary metabolites:

• Tannins (condensed and hydrolysable): High concentrations in bark and root, consistent with astringent antidiarrhoeal and wound-healing activity. Condensed tannins (proanthocyanidins) are particularly prominent.
• Saponins: Detected in bark, root, and seeds; potentially responsible for haemolytic and uterotonic activity reported in traditional use.
• Flavonoids: Including quercetin and kaempferol derivatives; associated with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects.
• Alkaloids: Detected in trace amounts in root bark; pharmacological significance uncertain.
• Polyphenolic acids: Gallic acid and ellagic acid reported in bark extracts.
• Sugars and mucilages: Abundant in fruit pulp; contribute to nutritional value and demulcent action.

Noted pharmacological studies (selected):
— Aqueous and methanolic bark extracts of D. microcarpum have demonstrated in vitro inhibitory activity against Shigella dysenteriae, Escherichia coli, and Salmonella typhi, providing microbiological support for the antidysenteric use (Ouédraogo et al., various CNRST Burkina Faso publications, 2000s; corroborated by Nigerian studies cited in Gill's Ethnobotanical Studies of Nigeria).
— Anti-inflammatory activity has been demonstrated in rodent models (carrageenan-induced paw oedema assay) using bark extracts.
— Antiplasmodial activity against Plasmodium falciparum has been reported in vitro for leaf and root bark extracts, though in vivo confirmation in animal models remains limited.
— The fruit pulp has been nutritionally characterised; ascorbic acid content measured at 10–40 mg/100 g fresh weight across multiple studies, with significant variation by ecotype and season.
— Hepatoprotective activity has been suggested in preliminary studies but requires confirmation.

CAUTIONS AND CONTRAINDICATIONS (as reported by traditional practitioners):
— Bark decoctions are generally contraindicated in pregnancy by more experienced practitioners, citing risk of miscarriage (consistent with uterotonic saponin content).
— High doses of the bark decoction are associated with nausea and vomiting; informants universally describe dosage as 'a small cup, not a large cup.'
— The seed kernel of D. microcarpum is reported as mildly toxic in large quantities; this contrasts with D. senegalense seed kernels, which are used as food in some areas.

--- SURVEY PROVENANCE ---
Data compiled from ethnobotanical surveys conducted in: Burkina Faso (Sissili, Zoundwéogo, Mouhoun Provinces); northern Ghana (Upper West, Northern, Upper East Regions); Nigeria (Sokoto, Kebbi, Kogi States); Senegal (Casamance, Sine-Saloum, Ferlo); Mali (Mopti, Ségou, Koulikoro Regions); Côte d'Ivoire (northern departments, Korhogo area). Survey periods: primarily 1985–2015, with some contemporary cross-referencing to 2020 literature.

1. SOURCE NOTE: The source segment is a directive/seed entry ('Write a comprehensive entry for this plant') rather than a transcribed primary ethnobotanical text. This corpus entry has therefore been constructed as a synthetic scholarly archival entry drawing on the documented ethnobotanical literature for Detarium microcarpum across the specified survey region. All specific claims are consistent with published ethnobotanical and pharmacological literature as of the mid-2020s, but should be verified against primary sources before citation.

2. SPECIES DISAMBIGUATION: The boundary between Detarium microcarpum and Detarium senegalense in the ethnobotanical literature is frequently blurred. D. microcarpum is the Sudano-Sahelian savanna species; D. senegalense is the Guinea-zone forest-margin species. Many older surveys (pre-1990) do not distinguish them reliably. This entry attributes uses primarily to D. microcarpum but notes where overlap is probable.

3. YORUBA NAME: The Yoruba name attribution is less firmly established in the consulted literature than the Hausa and Wolof designations. 'Oganwo igbo' appears in some Nigerian ethnobotanical compilations but requires verification; 'Tsamiyar yamma' is a Hausa-Yoruba contact-zone usage and may reflect borrowing rather than an indigenous Yoruba term.

4. PHARMACOLOGICAL CITATIONS: Specific author-year citations (e.g., Ouédraogo et al.) are representative of the literature but are not exhaustively verified for this entry; researchers should conduct independent literature searches via PubMed, AJOL (African Journals Online), and CNRST Burkina Faso institutional repositories.

5. TRADITIONAL CAUTIONS: Uterotonic and pregnancy-related cautions are reported from informant data and are pharmacologically plausible given saponin content; they do not constitute clinical recommendations.

Plant/Drug: Piliostigma thonningii — camel's foot — respiratory

West African traditional medicine — documented from ethnobotanical surveys across Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso. Write a comprehensive entry for this plant.
results.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY
Species: Piliostigma thonningii (Schumach.) Milne-Redh.
Family: Fabaceae (Caesalpinioideae)
Common Name: Camel's Foot; Monkey Bread [Anglophone West Africa]; Bauhinia thonningii [former synonym]
Primary Therapeutic Domain: Respiratory, antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, wound healing, gastrointestinal

---

LOCAL NAMES

• Yoruba (Nigeria): Abafe; Ako-igi-ajagbon
• Hausa (Nigeria/Niger/Mali): Kargo; Kalgo; Kalgori — the Hausa name 'kalgo' is widely documented across Sahelian survey literature and refers specifically to this species in northern Nigerian markets
• Twi (Ghana): Opentema; Ofuntumba
• Wolof (Senegal): Dantuman; Nguéweul
• Bambara/Dioula (Mali/Côte d'Ivoire/Burkina Faso): Niamankoro; N'golo
• Mooré (Burkina Faso): Bilga; Bilgo
• Fula/Fulfulde: Lekki-nyari

---

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION

Piliostigma thonningii is a deciduous to semi-deciduous shrub or small tree, typically reaching 3–10 metres in height, widespread across the savanna belt of sub-Saharan Africa from Senegal east through Sudan. It is most abundant in the Sudano-Sahelian and Guinea savanna zones. The leaves are characteristically bilobed at the apex — resembling a camel's or cow's footprint — a morphological feature that lends the plant its common English name. Bark is grey-brown, rough, and deeply furrowed on mature specimens. Flowers are white to pale pink with prominent veining, borne in short axillary or terminal racemes. Pods are woody, flat, indehiscent, reddish-brown at maturity, and persist on the tree, serving as an important dry-season fodder source.

---

THERAPEUTIC USES BY CONDITION AND REGION

1. RESPIRATORY CONDITIONS
   Condition(s) treated: Cough, bronchitis, asthma, chest congestion, whooping cough [pertussis]
   Plant part(s) used: Roots, stem bark, leaves
   Preparation method — Decoction: Stem bark (typically 50–100 g of dried bark per litre of water) is boiled for 20–30 minutes; the decoction is drunk warm, two to three times daily. In Hausa practice documented in northern Nigeria and Kano State surveys, the root bark decoction is specifically preferred for persistent cough and is sometimes combined with the dried fruit of Capsicum frutescens [red pepper] and honey to enhance expectorant action.
   Preparation method — Infusion: Dried leaves (a generous handful) are steeped in hot water for 10–15 minutes and drunk as a tea to relieve mild chest congestion and seasonal cough. This form is more commonly documented in Ghanaian (Twi-speaking) communities in Brong-Ahafo and Upper West regions.
   Preparation method — Steam inhalation: In Malian and Burkinabé practice, fresh leaves are added to boiling water and the steam is inhaled, with a cloth draped over the head, to relieve nasal and bronchial congestion.

2. WOUND HEALING AND DERMATOLOGICAL CONDITIONS
   Condition(s) treated: Wounds, ulcers, skin infections, boils, scabies, eczema
   Plant part(s) used: Leaves, stem bark
   Preparation method — Poultice: Fresh leaves are crushed or pounded into a paste, sometimes mixed with shea butter [Vitellaria paradoxa] or palm oil, and applied directly to wounds and infected skin lesions. Ethnobotanical surveys in Burkina Faso (Mooré-speaking communities) record this as a primary application for tropical ulcers and machete wounds.
   Preparation method — Wash/decoction: Bark decoction is used as a topical wash over skin infections; in Senegalese (Wolof) practice, the decoction is applied warm to boils to promote suppuration and drainage.

3. GASTROINTESTINAL CONDITIONS
   Condition(s) treated: Diarrhoea, dysentery, stomach pain, intestinal parasites [helminthiasis]
   Plant part(s) used: Roots, bark, leaves, pods
   Preparation method — Decoction: Root bark decoction is among the most widely documented antidiarrhoeal preparations in Hausa and Yoruba ethnomedicine. The high tannin content of the bark is considered pharmacologically consistent with its astringent [tannic] action on the intestinal mucosa.
   Preparation method — Powder: Dried pods or bark are ground into a powder that is mixed with water or porridge (tuwo or akamu) and consumed for intestinal complaints. Documented in Kano, Sokoto, and Kaduna State surveys (Nigeria).

4. FEVER AND MALARIA
   Condition(s) treated: Febrile illness, malaria [paludisme], general fever
   Plant part(s) used: Roots, bark
   Preparation method — Decoction: Administered orally; frequently combined with other anti-malarial plants such as Azadirachta indica [neem] bark or Khaya senegalensis [dry-zone mahogany] bark in poly-herbal preparations documented across the Sahel zone.

5. GYNAECOLOGICAL AND OBSTETRIC USES
   Condition(s) treated: Dysmenorrhoea [painful menstruation], post-partum bleeding, facilitation of labour [oxytocic use]
   Plant part(s) used: Roots, bark
   Preparation method — Decoction: Administered with caution; several survey respondents across Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana flagged potential abortifacient risk at high doses, consistent with documented uterotonic plant alkaloids in the Caesalpinioideae family. [TRANSLATOR'S NOTE: Safety flag — see pharmacological section below.]

6. ORAL HEALTH
   Condition(s) treated: Toothache, oral infections, gingivitis
   Plant part(s) used: Stem (chew sticks); bark
   Preparation method — Chew stick [dental hygiene]: Branches are widely used as chewing sticks [siwak-analogous practice] across northern Ghana, northern Nigeria, and Mali. The tannin and flavonoid content provides antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory benefit to oral tissues.

7. DIABETES AND METABOLIC CONDITIONS
   Condition(s) treated: Diabetes mellitus [locally described as 'sugar disease' or 'urine-that-attracts-ants']
   Plant part(s) used: Leaves, bark
   Preparation method — Decoction: Documented in multiple Ghanaian and Nigerian surveys; increasingly cross-referenced with laboratory studies showing hypoglycaemic activity (see Pharmacological Research below).

---

PHARMACOLOGICAL RESEARCH (SUPPORTING EVIDENCE)

A growing body of laboratory and preclinical studies has sought to validate the traditional uses of Piliostigma thonningii:

• Antimicrobial activity: Bark and leaf extracts have demonstrated inhibitory activity against Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, Salmonella typhi, and Candida albicans in in vitro assays. Zones of inhibition recorded in studies from University of Maiduguri (Nigeria) and University of Ghana, Legon, are consistent with the documented use of the plant for wound infections and dysentery.

• Anti-inflammatory activity: Methanol and aqueous extracts of P. thonningii bark showed significant reduction of carrageenan-induced paw oedema in Wistar rat models, supporting the use of bark decoctions for inflammatory conditions including arthritis and painful wounds.

• Antidiarrhoeal activity: Castor oil-induced diarrhoea models in rodents demonstrated a dose-dependent reduction in intestinal transit and stool frequency with P. thonningii bark extract, consistent with its high condensed tannin [proanthocyanidin] content.

• Hypoglycaemic activity: Streptozotocin-induced diabetic rat models showed significant reduction in fasting blood glucose following administration of aqueous leaf extracts, with effects attributed to flavonoid-mediated insulin sensitisation and alpha-glucosidase inhibition.

• Antioxidant activity: DPPH [2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl] radical scavenging assays confirmed moderate to high antioxidant capacity, attributed to phenolic acids, flavonoids (kaempferol, quercetin derivatives), and condensed tannins.

• Antipyretic activity: Yeast-induced fever models in rodents confirmed temperature-reducing effects of bark extracts, supporting traditional use for febrile illness.

• Phytochemical profile: Alkaloids, saponins, tannins (hydrolysable and condensed), flavonoids, terpenoids, and phenolic glycosides have been consistently identified across studies. The high tannin content accounts for astringent, antidiarrhoeal, and wound-healing properties. Saponins may contribute to expectorant activity documented in respiratory use.

• Uterotonic / reproductive caution: Preliminary studies indicate smooth-muscle stimulating compounds in root extracts; traditional practitioners across Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana explicitly caution against use in early pregnancy, a risk consistent with the alkaloid fraction. [Safety note retained for clinical relevance.]

---

ADDITIONAL NOTES

• Veterinary use: Extensively used across the Sahel for animal health — particularly for cattle diarrhoea, mange, and post-calving complications — reflecting the overlap between human and veterinary ethnomedicine in agropastoral communities.
• Food use: Young leaves are eaten cooked as a vegetable in parts of Mali and Burkina Faso. Ripe pods are palatable to livestock and sometimes used as emergency human famine food.
• Ecological role: A pioneer and nitrogen-fixing [symbiotic root nodule-forming] species important in land rehabilitation across degraded savanna landscapes.
• Conservation status: Not currently threatened; common across its range, though localised overcollection of bark for medicinal markets (documented in Kano and Kumasi herb markets) may warrant monitoring.

## Common and Local Names

- **English:** Nigerian bitters plant; red-fruited byrsocarpus; scarlet connaraceous shrub
- **Yoruba (Nigeria):** Igan-odan; Igan-igbo; Ewe-igan

## Botanical Description

*Byrsocarpus coccineus* Schum. & Thonn. is a scrambling shrub or liana belonging to the family **Connaraceae**. It typically attains a height of 1–4 metres, occasionally climbing to 8 metres when supported by adjacent trees. The stems are slender, pubescent when young, becoming glabrous and grey-brown with age. Leaves are alternate, pinnately compound, with 3–7 leaflets that are elliptic to obovate, glabrous or sparsely hairy on the lower surface, 4–12 cm long. The inflorescence is a terminal or axillary panicle bearing numerous small, fragrant, white to cream-coloured flowers. The fruit is a scarlet-to-orange follicle [pod-like structure], which splits at maturity to reveal a single shiny black seed partially enclosed in a bright orange-red aril [seed coat appendage] — a feature that gives the plant both its specific epithet (*coccineus*, meaning 'scarlet') and considerable ecological visibility. The plant favours forest margins, secondary bush, gallery forests, and savanna-woodland

results.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY

Plant: Waltheria indica Linnaeus (Family: Malvaceae / formerly Sterculiaceae)
Common vernacular synonym noted in source: malva de cien nudos [Spanish: 'mallow of a hundred knots'; a colonial-era or Lusophone-influenced trade name occasionally encountered in West African herbalist literature, likely carried through trans-Saharan or Atlantic trade routes]

PRIMARY THERAPEUTIC CONTEXT NOTED IN SOURCE: Epilepsy (attributed to Hausa-speaking communities, northern Nigeria and Niger borderlands)

---

LOCAL NAMES (documented across survey region):
• Hausa (Nigeria / Niger): 'yan gyatsa; also recorded as tsauri or gyadar daji in some northern Nigerian herbalist interviews
• Yoruba (Nigeria): ko-ira; occasionally rendered kóírà in tonal orthography; some sources record ewé àgbàdo igbó (field approximation)
• Twi (Ghana): nsempene or nsemsema; also ntorowa in Ashanti dialect zones
• Wolof (Senegal): mbep-mbep or mbepp; also sёbar bu dëkk in some Dakar-area surveys
• Bambara / Dioula (Mali / Côte d'Ivoire / Burkina Faso): wolowalé; also recorded as wolo-wolo in Bobo-Dioulasso market surveys
• Mooré (Burkina Faso): soalmde; also pиnga-pinga [ILLEGIBLE: diacritical uncertain in original field notes]
• Fula / Fulfulde: laɓɓuki or labuki (northern Burkina Faso and Mali surveys)

---

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION (for archival context):
Waltheria indica is a pantropical, erect to semi-prostrate perennial or annual herb or subshrub, typically reaching 0.3–1.5 m in height. Stems are stellate-tomentose [covered in star-shaped hairs], giving a characteristic silvery-grey or tawny appearance. Leaves are ovate to oblong, crenate-serrate [with rounded, saw-like margins], 2–9 cm long, pubescent on both surfaces. Flowers are small, yellow, five-petalled, clustered in dense axillary cymose heads. Fruit is a small obovoid capsule. The plant is a weed of roadsides, degraded farmland, savanna margins, and disturbed habitats throughout the Sudano-Sahelian and Guinea zones of West Africa. It is extremely common and is therefore widely accessible to rural healers.

---

THERAPEUTIC USES DOCUMENTED IN SURVEY REGION:

1. EPILEPSY AND CONVULSIVE DISORDERS
Condition: Epilepsy; febrile convulsions in children; seizure disorders generally referred to in Hausa ethnomedicine as farfadiya or ciwan farfaɗo.
Preparation method: Decoction. Roots (primary) or whole aerial parts are collected, washed, and boiled in water for 20–40 minutes. The resulting decoction is cooled, strained, and administered orally in measured doses (typically a calabash-full [approximately 200–300 mL] twice daily). In some Hausa practice documented in Kano and Sokoto states, the root decoction is combined with the bark of Ficus sycomorus and administered to adults experiencing tonic-clonic seizures.
Additional practice: In northern Burkina Faso (Mooré-speaking communities), the crushed fresh leaves are applied as a poultice to the forehead and temples of a convulsing child while the oral decoction is simultaneously prepared.

2. TOOTHACHE AND ORAL INFECTIONS
Condition: Toothache; dental abscess; gum inflammation.
Preparation method: Infusion or chewed root. Fresh roots are chewed directly and held against the affected tooth — the fibrous root material acts simultaneously as a mechanical cleanser [functioning as a chewing stick] and a local analgesic vehicle. Alternatively, a strong infusion (cold maceration overnight or brief boiling) is used as a mouth rinse. Documented in Wolof-speaking communities in Senegal (Thiès and Kaolack regions) and in Twi-speaking areas of Ghana (Brong-Ahafo and Ashanti regions).

3. FEVER AND MALARIA-ASSOCIATED SYMPTOMS
Condition: Fever; malaria (referred to variously as iba in Yoruba; saa in Twi; palu in Wolof pidgin usage).
Preparation method: Decoction of whole aerial parts. The entire above-ground plant (stems, leaves, flowers) is boiled in a large quantity of water; the patient is bathed with the warm decoction (external application) and also drinks a portion. This combined internal-external use is documented in Ghana (Volta Region) and Côte d'Ivoire (Boundiali and Korhogo districts).

4. SORE THROAT AND RESPIRATORY CONDITIONS
Condition: Sore throat; cough; bronchitis-like presentations.
Preparation method: Infusion. Leaves and flowers are steeped in hot water (infusion) for 15–20 minutes; honey or shea butter [karité] is sometimes added. Consumed as a warm tea two to three times daily. Documented in Mali (Ségou and Mopti regions) and northern Ghana.

5. SKIN CONDITIONS AND WOUND HEALING
Condition: Skin rashes; infected wounds; scabies-like dermatoses; boils.
Preparation method: Poultice. Fresh leaves are macerated or pounded in a mortar, sometimes mixed with shea butter or palm oil, and applied topically to affected areas. Alternatively, a leaf decoction is used to wash wounds. Documented across all six survey countries.

6. URINARY TRACT COMPLAINTS
Condition: Dysuria [painful urination]; urinary tract infection; gonorrhoea-associated urethral discharge.
Preparation method: Decoction of roots. Root material is boiled, strained, and drunk in quantities of approximately 200 mL three times daily until symptoms resolve. Documented in Nigeria (Oyo and Kwara states, Yoruba-speaking communities) and Senegal.

7. INFLAMMATION AND PAIN (GENERAL)
Condition: Arthritis; joint pain; general musculoskeletal pain.
Preparation method: Poultice of heated leaves applied to painful joints; concurrent oral decoction in some accounts. Documented in Burkina Faso and Mali.

---

PHARMACOLOGICAL AND PHYTOCHEMICAL RESEARCH (supporting literature summary):

The pharmacological study of Waltheria indica has expanded considerably since the 1990s and provides partial scientific corroboration for several of the traditional uses documented above.

• Alkaloids: The plant contains cyclopeptide alkaloids, notably waltherines A, B, C, and D, as well as adouetine Y and related compounds. These alkaloids have demonstrated CNS [central nervous system] activity in animal models, providing a plausible mechanistic basis for the anti-epileptic / anticonvulsant use. Studies published in Phytochemistry and the Journal of Natural Products (1970s–2000s, notably work by Tschesche, Brüning, and colleagues) isolated and characterised several of these compounds.

• Anticonvulsant activity: Extracts of W. indica roots have shown anticonvulsant properties in pentylenetetrazol (PTZ)- and maximal electroshock (MES)-induced seizure models in rodents, lending credibility to the Hausa and broader West African use in epilepsy management. (Ref: Ngo Bum et al., various publications ca. 2009–2015, Cameroon/West Africa pharmacology literature.)

• Anti-inflammatory and analgesic activity: Aqueous and methanolic extracts have demonstrated significant anti-inflammatory effects in carrageenan-induced paw oedema models and analgesic effects in acetic acid-induced writhing tests in mice. These findings support the documented uses in joint pain, fever, and wound inflammation.

• Antimicrobial activity: Extracts have shown activity against Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pyogenes, Escherichia coli, and Candida albicans in in vitro assays, supporting the wound-healing, oral-infection, and urinary-tract applications.

• Antipyretic activity: Consistent with the fever/malaria use, aqueous root extracts have demonstrated antipyretic effects in yeast-induced hyperthermia models in rodents.

• Antinociceptive properties: Consistent with the toothache application.

• Flavonoids and other compounds: The plant also contains flavonoids (including kaempferol and quercetin derivatives), tannins, saponins, and mucilaginous polysaccharides — the latter likely contributing to the emollient and wound-soothing properties of the poultice preparations.

• Safety profile: Toxicological studies to date suggest relatively low acute toxicity at therapeutic doses in animal models; however, long-term safety data are limited and no formal clinical trials in human populations have been published as of the survey period.

---

CONSERVATION AND AVAILABILITY NOTE:
Waltheria indica is not considered threatened; it is a pioneering weed species of high ecological resilience. Its availability in markets (notably in Yoruba, Hausa, Wolof, and Bambara herbalist stalls) is consistently recorded as good to abundant. It is not subject to over-harvesting pressures comparable to those affecting slower-growing medicinal trees in the region.

---

SOURCE ATTRIBUTION: Documented from ethnobotanical field surveys across Ghana, Nigeria, Senegal, Mali, Côte d'Ivoire, and Burkina Faso. Survey methodology included structured interviews with traditional healers [herbalists, bonesetters, and community health practitioners], market surveys, and cross-referencing with herbarium voucher specimens where available.

results.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY

Taxon: Ficus gnaphalocarpa (Miq.) A.Rich. ex Steud.
Family: Moraceae
Common/Trade Name: Mampala fig; woolly-fruited fig

LOCAL NAMES
• Yoruba (Nigeria): ìtàkùn ọpọtọ; occasionally referenced as ọ̀pọ̀tọ̀ igbó (forest fig)
• Hausa (Nigeria/Niger): gamji; gumjiya (variant recorded in Kano and Katsina surveys)
• Twi (Ghana): nwadua; dua-boro (bark-tree, descriptive)
• Wolof (Senegal): soump bi; mbamba (regional variant, Casamance surveys)
• Bambara/Dioula (Mali/Côte d'Ivoire/Burkina Faso): mampala (principal vernacular, source of trade epithet); n'galama (Burkina Faso, Moore-speaking communities)

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION
Ficus gnaphalocarpa is a large, semi-deciduous to evergreen tree of the family Moraceae reaching 15–25 m in height. It is characterised by a broad, spreading crown; aerial roots that may develop into buttresses in moist gallery forest; and smooth to lightly fissured grey-brown bark. Leaves are elliptic to ovate, 8–20 cm long, coriaceous [leathery in texture], with a distinctly hairy to tomentose [densely short-haired] abaxial [undersurface] surface — the specific epithet gnaphalocarpa ('woolly-fruited') refers to the pubescent [fine-haired] surface of the immature figs. Figs (syconia) are produced in axillary pairs, globose, 1–2 cm in diameter, reddish to orange-yellow at maturity, and bearing the characteristic woolly indumentum [surface hair covering] when young. The species is widely distributed across the Sudano-Guinean and Sudano-Sahelian zones, favouring riverine and gallery forest margins, woodland savanna, and villages where it is frequently retained as a shade or spiritual tree.

ETHNOBOTANICAL DOCUMENTATION

1. CONDITIONS TREATED
Primary indications recorded across survey regions:
• Diarrhoea and dysentery (most widely documented indication)
• Wound healing and topical infection management
Secondary/associated indications (recorded with lower frequency):
• Haemorrhoids
• Skin inflammation and dermatitis
• Fever (antipyretic use, documented in Mali and Burkina Faso)
• Urogenital complaints (dysuria; recorded in Senegal and Nigeria)
• Toothache and oral infections (bark chewing, recorded in Ghana and Côte d'Ivoire)
• Postpartum care (uterine toning; recorded in Hausa-speaking communities)

2. PLANT PARTS USED
• Stem bark (most frequently employed)
• Aerial roots (especially in wound applications)
• Leaf (topical poultice; less commonly in infusion)
• Latex (direct topical application to wounds and skin lesions)
• Fig/syconium (dietary; occasionally combined with medicinal preparations)
• Root bark (recorded in Mali for gastrointestinal complaints)

3. PREPARATION METHODS

a) DECOCTION (for diarrhoea and dysentery)
Stem bark is stripped from branches (trunk bark is less commonly harvested, reflecting conservation practice noted in several surveys). A handful of fresh or sun-dried bark pieces (approximately 50–100 g dry weight) is added to 1–2 litres of water and boiled over open fire for 20–40 minutes until the decoction [boiled aqueous extract] is reduced by roughly one-third and assumes a reddish-brown to tawny colour. The decoction is cooled, strained through cloth or basketwork, and administered orally. Dosage: one cup (approximately 200–250 ml) two to three times daily until symptoms resolve, typically 3–5 days. In Nigeria (Hausa practitioners), the decoction is occasionally combined with stem bark of Khaya senegalensis or Terminalia avicennioides to potentiate [enhance the therapeutic effect of] the preparation. In Mali, a pinch of natron [crude sodium carbonate/bicarbonate mineral salt] is sometimes added as a solubilising and palatability agent.

b) INFUSION (for fever and urogenital complaints)
Dried leaves or root bark are steeped [macerated in hot but non-boiling water] for 10–20 minutes. The infusion [aqueous extract prepared without prolonged boiling] preserves more volatile constituents than decoction. Administered orally as for decoction; also used as a wash for external urogenital symptoms in Senegal (Wolof practitioners).

c) COLD MACERATION (recorded in Côte d'Ivoire and Burkina Faso)
Bark or root is pounded to a paste and steeped in cold water overnight. Strained and drunk first thing in the morning for chronic gastrointestinal complaints. This method [termed macération à froid in Francophone survey records] is considered gentler and preferred for children and the elderly.

d) POULTICE (for wound healing and skin complaints)
Fresh bark or leaf is pounded with a pestle to a fibrous paste. The paste is applied directly to lacerations, ulcers, infected skin lesions, or inflamed areas and bound with a cloth dressing. Changed once or twice daily. Latex expressed directly from cut bark or young branches may be applied neat [undiluted] to small wounds and insect stings. In Ghana (Twi-speaking communities), leaf poultice is used specifically for dermatitis and fungal skin conditions.

e) BARK CHEWING / ORAL MASTICATORY USE (for toothache and oral infection)
Small slivers of fresh stem bark are chewed directly against the affected tooth and gum. The astringent [tannin-rich, tissue-contracting] juice is held in the mouth. Recorded in Ghana and Côte d'Ivoire.

f) POSTPARTUM PREPARATION (Hausa, Nigeria)
A mild decoction of stem bark, prepared at lower concentration than the antidiarrhoeal preparation, is administered to mothers in the early postpartum period for uterine involution [return of the uterus to pre-pregnancy size and tone] and to prevent postpartum haemorrhage. Precise dosage not systematically recorded in available surveys.

PHARMACOLOGICAL AND PHYTOCHEMICAL CONTEXT

Phytochemical screening of Ficus gnaphalocarpa stem bark and root bark has identified the following compound classes:
• Tannins (condensed and hydrolysable): consistent with the observed astringent properties and documented antidiarrhoeal and wound-healing efficacy
• Flavonoids (including quercetin and kaempferol glycosides): associated with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity
• Triterpenoids (lupeol, β-sitosterol, and related compounds): widely distributed across the genus Ficus; implicated in anti-inflammatory and membrane-stabilising effects
• Alkaloids (trace levels; reported in some Nigerian studies)
• Saponins: surface-active glycosides with potential antimicrobial and haemolytic properties
• Phenolic acids (gallic acid, ellagic acid derivatives)

Selected supporting research:
• Antidiarrhoeal activity: In vivo [in living organism] studies using castor-oil-induced diarrhoea models in rodents have demonstrated significant reduction in faecal output and frequency following oral administration of aqueous stem bark extract, consistent with the traditional use. The mechanism is attributed principally to tannin-mediated reduction of intestinal mucosal secretion and increased water reabsorption.
• Antimicrobial activity: In vitro [in laboratory conditions] disc-diffusion and broth-dilution assays have shown inhibitory activity against Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, Shigella dysenteriae, and Candida albicans — pathogens directly relevant to the diarrhoea and wound-infection indications.
• Wound-healing activity: Topical application of bark extract formulations has accelerated wound closure in excision and incision wound models in rats, corroborating the poultice tradition.
• Anti-inflammatory activity: Carrageenan-induced rat paw oedema models have confirmed anti-inflammatory effects, supporting fever and dermatitis indications.
• Antipyretic activity: Yeast-induced hyperthermia models in rodents have shown statistically significant temperature reduction following bark extract administration.

Note: The majority of pharmacological studies reference the broader Ficus genus or closely related species (e.g., Ficus sycomorus, Ficus sur); species-specific peer-reviewed studies on F. gnaphalocarpa remain limited, and direct extrapolation should be treated with appropriate caution pending further species-resolved investigation.

CONSERVATION AND PRACTICE NOTES
The species is not currently considered threatened across its range but is subject to localised bark-harvesting pressure near settlements. Practitioners in several survey communities (notably Ghana and Burkina Faso) reported traditional protocols limiting harvest to lateral branches rather than trunk bark, consistent with sustainable management practice. The tree holds spiritual significance in several communities (associated with ancestor veneration and community assembly sites), which may incidentally confer protection.

TOXICOLOGY AND SAFETY
No acute toxicity has been recorded in available ethnobotanical literature at traditional dosage levels. High-dose experimental administration of crude extracts in rodent models produced no mortality at doses up to 2000 mg/kg body weight (oral), suggesting a relatively safe therapeutic index [ratio of toxic to effective dose] at conventional use levels. Latex may cause contact irritation in sensitive individuals. Prolonged or very high-concentration use is not documented in traditional practice and is not recommended pending further safety characterisation.
